mission.title: Install and Configure Parsec on Kubernetes
mission.description: PARSEC is an open-source initiative that provides a common API for hardware security and cryptographic services in a platform-agnostic way. Installing PARSEC allows you to manage keys and perform cryptographic operations without needing to know the underlying hardware details.

steps.title: Add the Parsec Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Parsec Helm repository to your Helm configuration. This allows you to install Parsec using Helm charts.
```bash
helm repo add parallaxsecond https://parsec.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Parsec using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install the Parsec service using Helm. Make sure to create a namespace for it.
```bash
helm install parsec parallaxsecond/parsec --namespace parsec --create-namespace --version 1.4.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Parsec pods are running successfully in the parsec namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace parsec
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for local access
steps.description:
If you want to access the Parsec service locally, set up port forwarding:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/parsec --namespace parsec 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Parsec will show the Parsec pods in the 'Running' state when you execute `kubectl get pods --namespace parsec`. You should also be able to access the service locally via port 8080.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add parallaxsecond https://parsec.github.io/charts
helm repo update

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the Parsec installation, first uninstall the Helm release using the following command. This will remove the deployment and associated resources managed by Helm.
```bash
helm uninstall parsec --namespace parsec
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Next, remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volumes that may have been created during the installation. Use the following commands to delete the CRDs and any persistent volume claims (PVCs).
```bash
kubectl delete crd parsec.*
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace parsec
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the namespace used for Parsec to ensure all resources are cleaned up. Verify that the namespace has been removed successfully.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace parsec
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up the current state of your Parsec installation. You can do this by exporting the current configuration and resources.
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace parsec -o yaml > parsec-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts, then run the upgrade command to update your Parsec installation to the latest version.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade parsec parallaxsecond/parsec --namespace parsec --version 1.5.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the Parsec pods are running the new version successfully. Check the status of the pods in the parsec namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace parsec
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If your Parsec pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for the pod to diagnose the issue. This may indicate a misconfiguration or resource limits being too low.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace parsec
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Parsec service, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly and that the service is running. Check the service status with:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace parsec
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter errors related to insufficient resources, check the resource usage and limits set for the Parsec deployment. You can view the current resource usage with:
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace parsec
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you receive an error stating that the Helm release is not found, ensure that you are using the correct namespace and release name. List all Helm releases in the namespace with:
```bash
helm list --namespace parsec
```

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
